title: Unable to su as root to any other user - RHEL 6.9
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->
When running a container, after creating a user I am unable to su to that user as root. I will simply receive an su: incorrect password. I installed syslog to see if there were any errors and I had received:

su: pam_rootok(su:auth): authentication succeeded
su: PAM audit_log_acct_message() failed: Operation not permitted

I believe the second line is due to not having audit installed, but I had seen in various other posts that auditd does not work with docker containers (I am not sure if that is also the case with podman) and was unable to get auditd to work in my container.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Pull a RHEL 6.9 image

2. Run container "podman run -ti IMAGE_ID /bin/bash"

3. Create user "useradd usera"

4. Here I've attempted two things:
i. passwd usera -> this results in a "passwd: System error" but it appears as though /etc/shadow has changed.
ii. I map the host's /etc/shadow to the container, since I have the same users in both I'd expect that the passwords would work.

5. su to usera "su usera"

**Describe the results you received:**
su: incorrect password

**Describe the results you expected:**
Switching users to usera

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
After creating usera and saving as a new image, I was able to run the container as usera and su to both root and usera when prompted with a password. This worked when using my host's /etc/shadow mapped to the container. However, as root I was still unable to su to usera. I've attempted turning off SELinux on both the host and container, and disabling firewalls on both host and container. I attempted to bypass PAM by including "skip-authentication" at the beginning of /etc/pam.d/su. I've checked to make sure /bin/su has proper permissions. I ran both pwck and grpck to verify /etc/passwd and /etc/group. I checked to make sure usera is using /bin/bash and not /bin/nologin in /etc/passwd. I am just quite perplexed and hadn't been able to find any mentions of su as root in relation to containers, so I was curious if this is a container issue or a me issue.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 4.0.2
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out, @sentry124.

The following article should answer your question: https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/sudo-rootless-podman

Since you are using RHEL, I want to encourage to go through the Red Hat customer channels for issues in the future.  This allows for prioritizing your issues.

Hey @vrothberg,

Thanks for the reply, I will attempt to post there. However, that article does not appear relevant to my issue. I am not running a rootless container, and that is referring to starting the container, not attempting to use su within a running container itself (unless I am possibly misunderstanding the article).

Apologies, @sentry124.

@rhatdan may be able to help.

It appears to be an issue exclusive to RHEL 6 containers. I grabbed an ubuntu and RHEL 7.6 container and they appeared to function as expected. I am not sure if there is anything that can be done on your guys' end, but any feedback would be appreciated.
Does it work if you run in --privileged mode?

Adding that flag allows for su as root; however, I am trying to avoid running in privileged mode. Is there a limitation with RHEL6 that requires privileged mode in order to su? Or do you happen to know why RHEL 6 has this issue, but other OS's do not?

I am just trying to figure out what is blocking su.

I would now try 

--cap-add=all without --privileged.

If this does not work try with SELinux disabled.

--security-opt label=disabled

And try with SECCOMP disabled

--security-opt seccomp=unconfined

It might be

podman run --cap-add AUDIT_WRITE ...
Both --cap-add=all and --cap-add AUDIT_WRITE allowed for su without having to disable either SELinux or SECCOMP
This means that RHEL6 su wants to do AUDIT_WRITE or fails.  Thus add the capability and you are all set.  Newer versions ignore this error or do not attempt.
